questions.question:
A system of four concurrent processes, P, Q, R and S, use shared resources A, B and C. The sequences in which processes P, Q, R and S request and release resources are as follows:

Process P:  1. P requests B
            2. P requests B
            3. P releases A
            4. P releases B

Process Q:  1. Q requests C
            2. Q releases A
            3. Q releases C
            4. P releases A

Process R:  1. R requests B
            2. R requests C
            3. R releases B
            4. R releases C

Process S:  1. S requests A
            2. S requests B
            3. S releases A
            4. S releases C

i. Will the processes always get into a deadlock? If your answer is no, give a sequence of these operations which leads to completion of all processes.
ii. Can the system of four processes get into a deadlock in a system of concurrent processes using shared resources if preemption of granted resources is not allowed?
iii. What strategies can be used to prevent deadlocks in a system of concurrent processes using shared resources if preemption of granted resources is not allowed?

A. Yes, processes always deadlock; and deadlock prevention requires resource ordering
B. No, processes do not always deadlock; they can complete without deadlock if scheduled correctly; deadlock prevention strategies include resource ordering and Banker's algorithm
C. Yes, deadlock is inevitable without preemption; only detection and recovery works
D. No deadlock is possible since resources are released before others are requested
questions.answer: B. No, processes do not always deadlock; they can complete without deadlock if scheduled correctly; deadlock prevention strategies include resource ordering and Banker's algorithm
questions.to_find: Whether the four concurrent processes always deadlock, if not give a safe execution sequence, and what strategies prevent deadlock when preemption is not allowed.

understand.plain: Four processes P, Q, R, S share three resources A, B, C. Each process follows a fixed sequence of requesting and releasing resources. If a resource is free it is immediately granted; no preemption is allowed. We need to determine if deadlock is inevitable, provide a safe sequence if not, and identify deadlock prevention strategies.

keywords.explain: A situation where a set of processes are each waiting for a resource held by another process in the set, forming a circular wait — no process can proceed.
keywords.example: P holds A and waits for B; Q holds B and waits for A — neither can proceed.

keywords.explain: An ordering of processes such that each process can obtain all its needed resources (from available + released by earlier processes) and complete.
keywords.example: If S runs first, releases A and C; then P can get A and B; then Q and R can finish.

keywords.explain: Assign a global order to all resources; require every process to request resources only in increasing order. This breaks circular wait.
keywords.example: If order is A < B < C, no process can request A after already holding B or C.

keywords.explain: A deadlock avoidance algorithm that checks whether granting a resource request will leave the system in a safe state before actually granting it.
keywords.example: If granting R's request for C would leave the system in an unsafe state, the OS defers the grant.

understand.visual_alt: Four process boxes (P, Q, R, S) at top connected by dashed lines to three resource boxes (A, B, C) below, showing which processes share which resources.

given.aim: Determine if the four concurrent processes always deadlock, find a safe execution sequence if they do not, and identify deadlock prevention strategies.

terms.term: Four processes: P, Q, R, S
terms.meaning: Concurrent processes each following a fixed ordered sequence of resource request and release operations.
terms.example: Process P first requests B, then requests B again, then releases A, then releases B.
terms.connects: The interleaving of these operations determines whether deadlock can occur.

terms.meaning: Shared resources with (assumed) one instance each; a resource is granted immediately if free, not preemptible once granted.
terms.example: If B is held by P, then R's request for B must wait.
terms.connects: With one instance each, any circular hold-and-wait among processes leads to deadlock.

terms.meaning: Once a resource is granted to a process, it cannot be forcibly taken away; the process must voluntarily release it.
terms.example: If R holds C and P needs C, P must wait until R releases C.
terms.connects: No preemption is one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock; strategies must address the other conditions.

terms.meaning: An interleaved ordering of process steps such that every process eventually completes without waiting indefinitely.
terms.example: Running all of S's steps first frees A and C, allowing P and Q to proceed.
terms.connects: If a safe sequence exists, the system does not always deadlock.

given.plan: First, try to construct a safe execution order where all processes complete without waiting. If such an order exists, the answer to (i) is no. Then identify which of the four deadlock conditions are avoidable to answer (iii): since no-preemption is fixed, we must break mutual exclusion (not always possible), hold-and-wait, or circular wait via resource ordering or Banker's algorithm.

steps.title: Attempt a safe execution sequence
steps.formula_raw: Deadlock requires all four conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
steps.apply: Try the order: S1 (S requests A — granted), S2 (S requests B — granted), S3 (S releases A), S4 (S releases C — S completes). Now A is free, B is free. Run P1 (P requests B — granted), P2 (P requests B again — already held by P or trivially granted), P3 (P releases A), P4 (P releases B — P completes). Now run Q1 (Q requests C — granted), Q2 (Q releases A), Q3 (Q releases C — Q completes). Finally run R1 (R requests B — granted), R2 (R requests C — granted), R3 (R releases B), R4 (R releases C — R completes). All four processes complete successfully.
steps.note: Since a safe execution sequence S -> P -> Q -> R exists where no process waits indefinitely, the system does NOT always deadlock. The answer to part (i) is NO.

steps.title: Analyze whether deadlock is possible (part ii)
steps.formula_raw: Circular wait: P holds X waits Y, Q holds Y waits Z, R holds Z waits X
steps.apply: Consider an interleaving: P requests B (held), Q requests C (held), R requests B — waits (P holds B), then P releases A but needs to release B; however if P also waits for something held by Q or R, a cycle forms. For example: P holds A requests B; R holds B requests C; Q holds C requests A — this is a circular wait and a deadlock. So YES, deadlock IS possible under some interleavings.
steps.note: Part (ii) answer: Yes, the system CAN get into a deadlock under certain unfortunate interleavings, even though it does not ALWAYS deadlock.

steps.title: Deadlock prevention strategies (part iii)
steps.formula_raw: Prevention: eliminate at least one of the four necessary conditions
steps.apply:
Since preemption is not allowed, we cannot break the no-preemption condition. Strategies available are:
1. Resource Ordering: Assign a global order to resources (e.g., A < B < C). Require every process to request resources only in increasing order. This eliminates circular wait.
2. Hold-and-Wait Prevention: Require a process to request all its resources at once before starting. No process holds a resource while waiting for another.
3. Banker's Algorithm (Deadlock Avoidance): Before granting any request, check whether the resulting state is safe. Only grant the request if it leads to a safe state.
steps.note: Resource ordering is the simplest static prevention technique. The Banker's algorithm is a dynamic avoidance approach. Both are valid answers for part (iii).

solution.result: i. No, the processes do NOT always deadlock. A safe sequence is S -> P -> Q -> R. ii. Yes, deadlock IS possible under certain interleavings (circular wait can form). iii. Prevention strategies: resource ordering (impose global request order to eliminate circular wait), hold-and-wait elimination (request all resources at once), or Banker's algorithm for deadlock avoidance.

questions.formula_note: Deadlock necessary conditions (Coffman conditions): mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait. Deadlock prevention eliminates one condition; deadlock avoidance (Banker's algorithm) ensures the system stays in a safe state.
